data.title: International Flavors & Fragrances Q2: A Good Business With A Bad Balance Sheet
data.description:
I’ve looked at International Flavors & Fragrances Inc. (NYSE:IFF) many times in the past few years, but it never quite made it to my watchlist. Not because I thought it was a bad company, but because IFF is one of those businesses that looks perfect at first glance with a solid brand, recurring demand, high gross margins, and a huge presence across industries that are part of all of our day-to-day life.

The problem is that every quarter, the numbers come in, there’s some cautious optimism from the bulls, maybe a little stock appreciation, and then the stock tanks back down. Over the last year, IFF has traded like a company stuck in a loop. And after going through their latest earnings, I think all of this is because the business itself is great, but the balance sheet is a mess.

Company and Earnings

If you are unsure about what the company does, the best explanation can be found on the main page of this company, located here on Seeking Alpha:

International Flavors & Fragrances Inc., together with its subsidiaries, manufactures and markets food, beverage, health and biosciences, scent, pharma solutions, and complementary adjacent products in the United States, Europe, and internationally. It operates in four segments Nourish, Health & Biosciences, Scent, and Pharma Solutions.

At first glance, Q2 2025 didn’t look bad at all. Sales were up slightly (1% year-over-year), gross margins expanded nicely to 36.2%, and adjusted EBITDA rose 6%. The company reaffirmed full-year guidance and finally brought its net leverage down to 2.5x, which, to their credit, is something they’ve been working hard to achieve.

The problem is that while operationally things are clearly improving, the bottom line is still not improving as much. On a (Non-GAAP) adjusted basis, IFF posted $185 million in net income, which is a solid number, no doubt. But when you compare it against $3 billion in quarterly revenue, you’re still looking at modest profitability. The bigger problem is free cash flow, which came in at just $94 million, which isn’t nearly enough to cover capex and dividends. So even with the income statement finally is in the black, the cash flow statement shows that everything isn’t so great yet.

But, at the same time, I really like IFF’s core business. This is the type of company we barely hear about, but they are responsible for making ingredients, flavors, enzymes, and scents for everything from food to cosmetics to pharmaceuticals, that we use all the time in completely different products. It can be in your toothpaste, your shampoo, your yogurt, your medicine. They sell to tens of thousands of customers in 65+ countries.

Segments

Their business is divided into segments, and the Taste business is the winner. In Q2, it posted a nice 6% sales gain driven by Latin America and Europe, following another solid quarter in Q1 with about 7% growth. It brought in $631 million in sales this quarter. EBITDA margin came in at 19.8%, which was slightly lower than a year ago (20.4% in Q2 2024).

Food Ingredients is also improving after a restructuring. This segment brought $850 million in net sales just this quarter. EBITDA margin rose to 14.6%, from 12.6% a year ago, which is great, as it means that the pricing actions are successful.

Scent showed a mixed picture with 1% growth in net sales, but 2% decline in operating EBITDA - Operating Margin shrank from 23.4% to 21.6% on a year-over-year basis. Fine Fragrance is growing (high single digits), but Fragrance Ingredients are still under pressure. Management is shifting the portfolio toward higher-value molecules, but that will take time. The same story goes for Health & Biosciences, once considered the high-margin growth machine, now in a soft patch due to weak demand and inventory destocking. It’s still the strongest segment in terms of operating margin (26.2% currently), but it declined from the same quarter last year (it was 26.8%).

The surprise winner this quarter was Pharma Solutions. It’s a small segment, just $103 million in sales this quarter, but it grew 21% year-over-year, making it IFF’s fastest-growing business by far. However, operating margin in this segment saw the steepest decline of all, falling from 25.3% to 21.4%. So while volume and demand are clearly surging, the business is becoming less profitable. That’s not necessarily a red flag, in high-growth phases, margins often compress, but it’s something worth watching.

So, IFF has strong segments and healthy operational margins, but all of this is not enough for the net income to be as impressive.

So Why Did The Stock Drop 6%?

At a certain point, the market’s patience starts to wear off, and this is probably what happened to IFF this quarter. IFF didn’t miss expectations this quarter, they didn’t even guide lower. But the stock still tanked because “it’s getting better” is just not enough anymore.

The financials aren’t terrible, but they’re not compelling, either. Free cash flow is positive, about $94 million year-to-date, but it’s not enough to fund both internal investment and dividend payments. Operating cash flow is higher, around $368 million, which helps, but it still doesn’t show the serious turnaround everyone hopes to see.

And then there’s the balance sheet. Gross debt is down significantly, which is great, but we’re still looking at $6.2 billion in debt and just $816 million in cash. That’s a lot of pressure for a business that’s still rebuilding confidence. Don’t get me wrong, IFF can pay its bills, but this isn’t the kind of setup that gets investors excited to buy the stock.

Besides, the management has made it clear that 2025 is a transition year. The big recovery is not expected to show up until 2026. So, Wall Street is looking at a stock with no clear near-term catalyst, soft free cash flow, and a business model that’s still digesting past mistakes.

We have strengthening commercial and R&D pipelines that I am confident will begin to show more impact in 2026 and build for full benefit in 2027.

We are clear on our strategy, are getting stronger at commercial innovation, production and productivity execution, and we'll get through this as we build strength for 2026 and beyond.

By the way, the market’s reaction was twofold. Investors sold the news first, then realized the worst didn’t happen, and maybe even a few good things are going to get out of it, so the stock started its recovery after the market closed. After all, IFF reiterated full-year guidance, leverage is improved significantly, and the company announced a $500 million share repurchase plan. It could have been enough to attract some buyers, especially quant funds.

Valuation: It’s Not Cheap

You might think that with all this drama, IFF would be trading like a distressed asset. Well, at least this is what I expected to see pressing on the valuation tab. But it’s not. The stock sits around 16.5x forward earnings (on an adjusted basis), and almost 3x forward PEG (price to earnings divided by growth). That’s higher than you’d expect for a company with financial struggles.

Gross margins are high, but the return on equity is negative. Free cash flow margin is still in the red. The fact that IFF is investing and restructuring, together with paying interest, really hurts its bottom line. To me, this is a stock that looks "not terrible," but isn't priced for the level of risk it still carries. It's not expensive, but it’s definitely not screaming “deep value.”

What Needs to Happen Next

At this point, the path forward isn’t rocket science. IFF knows what it needs to do, and it seems like the market knows what to expect. First, they have to keep pushing margins higher in the Taste and Food Ingredients segments. That’s where the pricing power is, and where the company still has a chance to build consistency. Then there’s Health & Biosciences, the segment that’s still a bit messy. The restructuring is well underway, but volume recovery isn’t there yet.

Debt is another problem, even though the leverage is coming down. Their balance sheet really needs a reduction in total debt, because interest expense is still eating up too much of the cash flow. And speaking of cash flow, they need more of it. At least enough to pay the dividend.

Conclusion

IFF is a good company. I honestly believe that. The problem is that, for the past few years, it was run more like a private equity firm than a public company. Now they’re trying to fix it, and they’ve made some progress. But unless you're incredibly patient, I don't think this is the perfect stock to invest in. There is no clear upside catalyst until late 2025 or 2026, and until then, I would stay aside. Especially, it’s smart because the stock is in a negative momentum.

data.title: Rivian Q2: Another Quarter Of Disappointment
data.description:
Rivian: Challenges Galore...

Well, it looks like I should have persevered with my past bearish rating on the stock of Rivian Automotive, Inc. (NASDAQ:RIVN), but I had higher hopes that its execution could improve. Yet, somehow management demonstrated why the woes of the subscale EV manufacturer is far from over, pressured by President Trump's less generous regulatory regime on EVs, while also being hampered by the current rare earth export control issues with China. Hence, the EV maker's efforts to make critical capacity adjustments to prime the production for its lower-priced R2 platform has faced an untimely snafu.

In my previous RIVN writeup, I raised several persistent challenges that have exacted a frustrating toll on the bullish sentiments of the stock. While the market appears to have reflected its relatively tepid deliveries/production scorecard in early July, the market probably realized that the flow of bad news may not quite have ended yet.

Nevertheless, Rivian believes that its profitability trajectory toward 2027 shouldn't be materially affected. Yet, the need for management to lower its optimism for FY2025 has offered investors a sobering admonition about the high ambiguities concerning Rivian's near-term outlook. As a reminder, Rivian posted an automotive gross loss of $335M, emanating from the fixed cost under absorption at its current significantly underutilized Illinois facility (plausibly no more than 20% utilization based on nameplate capacity).

Rivian's Profitability Roadmap Faces A Murky Outlook

Hence, the less than 6K in units produced for Q2 really hampered the company's near-term profitability performance, resulting in an impact of $14K per unit, while also needing to account for higher LCNRV related costs in the same quarter. With that in mind, I'm not surprised that management decided to rein in expectations on Rivian's 2025 adjusted EBITDA outlook, while still needing to carry out retooling work in Q3 to prime for R2 manufacturing.

Note that the revised guidance indicates a range of between -$2B and -$2.5B in adjusted EBITDA, a decline from the previous range of between -$1.7B and -$1.9B. Also, with the significant reduction in regulatory credits revenue growth prospects, the market must also consider whether Rivian's gross margins accretion could be further affected since these aren't cyclical headwinds.

I think herein lies the problem for subscale EV makers like Rivian. Of course, we know that the entry-level price of $45K for R2 should theoretically expand access to a broader customer segment, but also competing more aggressively with Tesla (TSLA). And although the EV transformation plans by the legacy automakers are assessed to have been scaled back, even Tesla's recent performance shows that the overall market environment is expected to remain challenging.

Hence, even with a lower price point, it doesn't "magically" help Rivian gain to bolster demand dynamics significantly, justifying the increase in production footprint for R2. Note that Rivian has earmarked 155K units for R2 production. However, I believe astute investors comprehend that Rivian's underutilization has cost it dearly in terms of fixed costs leverage, hampering the accuracy in its gross profits forecasts.

Still the company indicates that it retains robust support from Volkswagen (OTCPK:VWAGY) (OTCPK:VLKAF) (OTC:VLKPF) as a key financing partner and investor. Yet, I think the market will increasingly focus on execution moving ahead, even if the liquidity challenges are less arduous as compared to the days it had to navigate with Volkswagen liquidity injection.

With more than $7.5B in current cash and equivalents (not including a further $2.5B in incremental capital from Volkswagen, and the possible $6.6B DoE loan), I don't think Rivian is in an imminent liquidity crisis that could have caused even more consternation on the part of investors.

Rivian's Cash Burn Must Be Monitored Closely

Despite that, Rivian is still estimated to burn through $8.4B in cash flow between 2025-27. Hence, as long as the incremental milestones agreed with Volkswagen are met, it seems like the company might just scrape through, if it could improve upon its production/deliveries metrics from 2026.

But, that remains a big if for now, as the sales cadence for R2 remains untested, while the industry is circumventing cyclical headwinds that have impacted the overall EV market. And the tariff-related impacts might still percolate through Rivian's P&L over the next couple of years, although management emphasized that the planning process in the second half should be less murky as compared to the first half.

Yet, we have already observed just how challenging it's to make accurate prognosis, amidst the current challenges hampering Rivian's supply chain. Hence, Rivian investors seeking more clarity into its production cadence from 2026 might conceivably not have the answers they need until the latter part of this year.

Buy Or Sell RIVN Stock?

With that in mind, I don't expect RIVN buyers to return with the level of conviction that saw the stock re-test the $17 level in May 2025. As seen above, the stock has since collapsed from that peak, with this week's selloff also eclipsing the July lows. Unprofitable EV stocks like RIVN has had a very grueling year in 2025, dealing with the policy flipflops of the Trump administration, while steering past frustrating industry challenges and supply chain snag.

And, the market has little "sympathy" for poor performance from growth-related plays like RIVN. But, that's how it should be. We are all here to invest profitably, and Rivian has yet to convince the market that it can accelerate its route to profitability (in contrast, it seems to have backtracked with its lowered guidance).

With the stock inching closer to a possible support zone at the $10.5 level, keeping my hold rating seems apt, while we allow the market action to pan out.

Rating: Maintain Hold.

data.title: VPLS: Beyond The Core In Fixed Income
data.description:
Introduction

In today's article, we will analyse the Vanguard Core-Plus Bond ETF (NASDAQ:VPLS) exchange-traded fund investment vehicle, which operates under the umbrella of the investment management company Vanguard. This fund is actively managed and seeks to provide an acceptable level of return while generating steady cash flows, operating in the US fixed income market.

Its benchmark is the Bloomberg U.S. Universal Total Return Index, which includes government bonds, corporate bonds, mortgage-backed securities, and some foreign debt. The fund managers use active management (‘core-plus’), which starts with allocating the fund's capital to treasuries or investment-grade corporate bonds as the “core” part of the strategy, leaving the ‘plus’ for high-yield bonds with lower credit quality, thereby seeking extra returns. In addition to this, managers often use derivatives, such as interest rate futures or swaps, to adjust the fund's various positions and improve both its exposure and risk management.

Its exposure range is 80% in treasury bonds or investment-grade bonds under normal market conditions, leaving a ceiling of 20% of the total capital managed by the fund for investments in high-yield bonds. The fund earns money either through monthly coupon payments on the bonds in its portfolio or through the appreciation of these bonds over time. The main risks associated with this investment would be interest rate risk, as a change in the yield curve would alter the NAV and the fund's return accordingly. Credit risk, caused by widening or rating downgrades, and liquidity risk, as mortgage securities are less liquid and may incur higher trading costs.

In the following images, the reader will find two images showing the main characteristics of the fund and its distribution broken down by instrument and credit quality. As the reader can see, as of 30 June this year, the vehicle analysed has 1,808 securities in its portfolio, compared to the more than 20,000 held by its benchmark index. This could initially appear to be a red flag if it were a passively managed vehicle, but as the fund managers actively manage the fund, we do not consider it to be a cause for concern. The vehicle has sufficient securities to be well diversified and achieve its target return.

Its average effective maturity is 8.6 years, compared to 8.0 years in the index, while its duration is 5.7 years (5.8 years in the benchmark). This average duration places the fund in the ‘medium’ range of the interest rate sensitivity scale, balancing the potential for gains in the event of falling interest rates with protection against moderate increases.

45.7% of assets are invested in US Treasury debt and federal agencies. The remaining 46.6% is divided between investment grade (AAA–BBB totalling 41.6%) and below-investment grade credit (BB–CCC, with 9.8%). Only 0.6% falls into CCC or below, which gives clues as to the caution of the management team in expanding exposure to high yield in order to achieve that aforementioned ‘plus’ in returns. Finally, in this section, it should be noted that its turnover rate reaches 471.6%, reflecting the intensity of active management and constant adjustment to changing credit and interest rate conditions.

Analysis – Macro Fundamental

In this section, we will analyse some macroeconomic indicators in detail to try to offer the reader an accurate perspective and a sound recommendation on the vehicle in question.

The first metric presented is the implied forward rates of the Treasury curve. This reflects market expectations regarding future interest rates at different maturity intervals. The graph shows lines such as ‘1 M→3 M’, ‘3 M→6 M’, and ‘1 Y→2 Y’, which indicate the rate at which financing can be agreed today for those future horizons. As of today, the slope of the short maturities (1 M→3 M and 3 M→6 M) has been declining from highs in May 2024 of around 5.5% to levels of around 4.3%, while the long maturities (5 Y→10 Y and 10 Y→30 Y) are around 4.6–5.1%, following a recent small rebound.

This relative flattening of the forward curve implies that the market is discounting rate cuts in the short term but remains more cautious about longer maturities. For a manager such as the one of the funds analysed, these signals serve as a guide for adjusting the effective duration: when the medium-long segment offers a higher carry and the market does not foresee sharp rises, increasing the duration in these areas somewhat can bring price gains and a higher implied coupon, but as we will see in the next metric, it may not be wise to do so without any measure, as we will see in the next indicator.

The second metric we analyse is the z-score of the exponentially weighted moving average (EWMA) of parameter β₁ of the Nelson-Siegel curve, which measures the slope of the yield curve. In practice, β₁ reflects how steep the curve is between short and long maturities; its change tells us whether the curve is steepening or flattening relative to its own past history.

As of today, this z-score is slightly below zero (around −0.5), after having gone through a very broad cycle: from peaks of around +3 in the first months of 2023—when the curve rose sharply after anticipating increases—to lows of almost −2 in early 2022 and again in early 2025. The current moderate negative value indicates that, in recent weeks, the slope has been flattening somewhat faster than usual, although without reaching market extremes. For fund managers, this signal is key when adjusting the portfolio structure based on the yield curve. This negative Z-score may suggest that it is no longer worthwhile to seek capital gains via steepening by overweighting the long end of the curve relative to the short end.

Therefore, managers could slightly reduce exposure to the medium- to long-term portion of the curve, offsetting this by opening positions in the short-term segment or adjusting the effective duration of the vehicle through the use of derivatives.

The third indicator shows, using a heat map of z-scores in 21-day windows, how credit spreads, term, liquidity, and high-yield spreads have performed relative to their historical average. Each coloured box reflects how many standard deviations the current spread is from its recent average: red colours indicate significant widening and blue colours indicate compression in the different spreads.

As of today, the different z-scores are around a positive value of 0.5 for high yield and corporate credit segments. For a fund of this nature, this moderate spread environment offers two interpretations. On the one hand, the extra coupon that managers obtain by overweighting credit remains attractive compared to the coupon currently paid by treasuries. In this environment, and with no signs of widening in the near future, we could conclude that it would be interesting to rotate part of the capital into credit bonds to capitalise on that extra coupon.

Conclusion

In conclusion, and to wrap up this article, I recommend keeping the analysed vehicle in your portfolio for several reasons. Forward rates point to an increase in the medium to long term, which could justify an increase in duration to capture that extra income. However, on the other hand, the second indicator shows that the curve is flattening faster than usual, which reduces the potential extra income that can be captured every day. Thank you for reading.

data.title: Schlumberger: Cash Flowing Through The Cycle Bottom
data.description:
Investment thesis

SLB, formerly Schlumberger (NYSE:SLB), isn't having a good year and is down 20% from January when I last reviewed it for Seeking Alpha. While broader energy services ETFs (XES) are also down, SLB has lagged behind Baker Hughes (BKR), a peer with less upstream exposure, and TechnipFMC (FTI), a subsea niche player with significant backlog:

Although the current oil bear market started in 2022, SLB's business model was seen as relatively resilient by analysts due to its low exposure to the volatile, short-cycle oilfield capex in the US. Long-cycle, international markets where SLB gets 80% of its revenue are considered more robust to oil price fluctuations.

However, over the last 12-18 months international oil producers have also cut spending, particularly in the Middle East (Saudi Aramco) and LATAM (Pemex). Consequently, SLB delivered lower numbers compared to Q2 2024. Even if this wasn't unexpected - the earnings were nominally a "beat" - the earnings call didn't suggest an imminent recovery in oilfield spend either.

On the brighter side and unlike prior downturns, SLB continues to generate ample free cash flow (or FCF) at about ~9% yield, implying the market is pricing in close to zero future growth. Energy services may indeed stagnate until 2026 or 2027, but long-term growth is virtually guaranteed by the 6-7% annual depletion rate for the average oilfield.

In the meantime, the 9% FCF yield covers the 3.4% dividend plus the share buybacks. SLB's dividend has also placed it among the holdings of the popular Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD). The shareholder returns alone may make SLB a reasonable investment to hold in flat energy services environment while awaiting the repricing once the cycle ultimately turns around.

SLB's recent performance was probably impacted too by the 15 months it took to close the ChampionX acquisition due to regulatory hurdles in the UK. The acquisition is fundamentally positive as it shifts SLB's revenue mix from oilfield "capex" (developing new oil production) to "opex" (maintaining existing production) and reduces the earnings volatility. The synergies guided by management aren't insignificant either.

Strategically, I continue to see SLB as a "buy." E&P customers may shift their spending timeline "to the right" but ultimately the longer capex is deferred, the bigger the depletion gap that will need to be filled. Tactically, though, it appears we may retest the April 9 lows, so it may be prudent to wait for the market's confirmation we aren't headed for a lower low. Currently I maintain long exposure through sold puts.

Q2 showed weakness in International

The energy services paradigm has shifted from 2023. Two years ago, the slowdown was in the US while International was still growing. However, as SLB's earnings show, the Middle East and Latin America are now also experiencing headwinds, despite the long-cycle nature of their production:

In fact, North America was (relatively) the best performing region year-on-year.

Worldwide, EBITDA appears to have peaked for now but it remains on par with 2023-2024:

EBITDA margins are also down though still high historically:

Unfortunately for SLB, the price-to-earnings multiple, which was factoring in quite a bit of growth, has now collapsed:

It now sits at a less aspirational 10-11x level. As a high-level calculation, if we assume SLB faces a 10% cost of capital, we could say the 10x multiple - equivalent to 10% earnings yield - prices in zero growth. The FCF yield indicates a similar conclusion.

CEO Le Peuch's Q2 comments implied a flat to low single digit second-half growth, so if that holds, the valuation correction should be close to done:

[To] be coming back to the guidance, I think the guidance reflects from the low to the high guidance, flat to LSD with potential to go even beyond this.

However, the call didn't appear to provide any clear indication either when SLB expects international growth to resume. For example, regarding the more resilient subsea and offshore markets Mr. Le Peuch commented:

[To] comment on what we have seen for the last 12 to 18 months. We have seen white space developing. And indeed, the least critical project to shift to the right. but we still have seen the rich pipeline of advantaged projects that are key for the portfolio of international operators, partly the IOCs to be going forward with anticipated FID.

"Shift to the right" refers to projects being postponed. While this is much better than projects being canceled altogether and the opportunity pipeline still looks good, there is no guarantee these projects won't be postponed yet again. In other words, while SLB management reiterated its long-term confidence regarding international markets, they didn't visibly commit to promising a sharp or "V-shaped" recovery.

My personal view though is the longer an "L-shaped" recovery takes, the sharper the capex rebound would ultimately be. A longer recovery also tends to favor companies that are cash positive at the cyclical lows and can wait out the downturn.

Key international markets may have bottomed

Baker Hughes' (BKR) widely tracked international rig count aligns to SLB's results. As of Q2 and apart from the US, the weakest markets were Latin America and the Middle East:

In Latin America, Mexico has been a source of weakness as the heavily indebted national oil company PEMEX has lacked the means to finance new developments.

Mexico's government, however, doesn't seem pleased with the oil production decline that has resulted from deferring investment. The government just recently, through a complex financial operation, effectively injected $12 billion into the company, and there are also plans to spur more investment by farming out stakes to private operators.

Since February-March Mexico's rig count is up 50% so perhaps the lows are behind us:

In the Middle East, Saudi Aramco has been reducing investment since it publicly gave up on its ambition to increase its "maximum sustainable capacity" for oil production from 12 to 13 million barrels per day. The shift in Aramco's plans also resulted in a large number of rig suspensions, both offshore and land, which reduces demand for SLB's services.

More recently, though, there are indications some Saudi rigs are coming back online. Overall, the Saudi rig count may be trying to stabilize around 70:

Outside of the US, Saudi Arabia and LATAM, management seemed fairly optimistic:

Regionally, the Middle East and Asia will continue to display the most resilience in the short term, driven by lower break-evens and a sustained focus on energy security. Meanwhile, advantaged offshore projects will lend support to the steady market across Europe, Africa and Americas. In contrast, land activity across North America and Latin America have the greatest downside risk due to short cycle spend.

Source: Management comments from Q2 call

The ChampionX acquisition has now closed

The long awaited close of the ChampionX acquisition may also be a catalyst. ChampionX, itself the product of a not so distant merger between Apergy and Ecolab's (ECL) energy upstream business, specializes in production chemicals and artificial lift technology. Unlike the drilling or completions businesses which depend on upstream capex, ChampionX's portfolio is geared towards production enhancement and optimization, which fall under operating expenditures.

The upstream energy business is notoriously capex intensive, with energy companies having to spend huge amounts of money to drill and complete wells. However, once the oil or gas is flowing, the capex is already a sunk cost and the operating expenses required to maintain production are much smaller in comparison. When faced with low oil prices, upstream companies may be quick to cut down on new capex, but they almost never shut existing production which makes upstream opex less volatile.

SLB's long-term view appears to be that oilfield capex will peak in the next 15-20 years, after which upstream spending would be dominated by opex:

With ChampionX, SLB is trying to position its portfolio for the coming changes:

Customers are in a quest to unlock and optimize the full production potential of their assets while improving efficiency in the reservoir recovery phase of their operations. This is creating a less cyclical and growing market opportunity that is more opex driven and is less sensitive to short-term commodity cycles.

Source: Management comments from Q2 call

In the near term, there is of course management's promise of non-trivial synergies which was reiterated on the Q2 call:

We are now even more comfortable with our initial assessment that we will be able to generate $400 million of annual pretax synergies within the first 3 years after closing.

SLB will start consolidating ChampionX into its results in August 2025, so Q3 will be the first quarter reflective of the combined business. As management's ability to provide guidance on combined basis has been limited, I would expect more significant revisions to analysts estimates once the restrictions are no longer in place.

The shareholder returns continue

SLB's shareholder returns have been resilient despite the industry-wide malaise in energy services:

In 2024, SLB spent $1.8 billion on share repurchases and paid out $1.5 billion in dividends, for a total distribution yield of ~7%.

So far in 2025, SLB has devoted $2.4 billion to repurchases, already exceeding its 2024 total. Per management's Q2 comments, more than half of the 141 million shares issued to finance the ChampionX acquisition have been offset:

Since the announcement of the ChampionX transaction in April 2024, we have been accelerating the repurchasing of our shares. To that end, since the announcement, we have reduced our total shares outstanding by 78 million. This represents 55% of the shares we just issued in this transaction.

The dividends continue at $0.285 per quarter, an increase of 3.6% from the $0.275 rate in 2024. The forward dividend yield is about 3.4% now. Assuming no further repurchases for the remainder of the year and ignoring the dividend on the incremental ChampionX shares, SLB would end 2025 with $4 billion returned, reflecting a ~8% total distribution yield.

Importantly, SLB's returns are 1) well covered by its FCF generation; 2) also covered at what is a low point for the oilfield services cycle. Analyst consensus implies a 9% FCF yield:

Management emphasized that capex will stay at 5% to 7% of revenue for the near term as the company remains mindful of its FCF conversion goals.

Equity analysts see significant potential

The price targets continue to slide but analysts seem to be increasing the implied upside percentage, now at 36%:

Frankly, the analyst bullishness on SLB with 19 of 29 ratings being "strong buy" worries me a bit as "too consensus":

SLB is indeed the largest player in the space, but energy services stocks rarely get so much "love" from the Street, especially in what has been a lackluster year for oil prices.

Over the next few quarters, I expect analysts to look for "tangible" evidence the promised ChampionX synergies are being realized. That may influence how these price targets evolve in the near term.

Risks to watch out for

The OPEC+ spare capacity remains a risk but probably less so as the "voluntary cuts" have pretty much been reversed. However, oil prices can also be pressured by demand weakness in the event the global economy slows down.

I am also cautious of the risk that upstream FIDs (final investment decisions) slide further "to the right", meaning that projects expected to commence in 2026 are postponed to 2027-2028. This could be driven not just by oil prices but also the general economic uncertainty, including tariffs, or supply chain bottlenecks as seen in the offshore segment where FPSOs (floating production storage and offloading units) faced some delays.

Takeaway

Despite the disappointing macro environment for energy services, SLB remains a high-quality company that is able to reward its shareholders even through the cyclical bottom. The downside is likely limited from here while the upside, when upstream capex inevitably returns, could be considerable.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 231: $23,100 Allocated, $2,461.19 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
This was a difficult week in the markets despite big tech blowing earnings out of the water. The Fed kept rates the same which didn’t shock a lot of people. Non-farm payrolls increased by 73,000 in July rather than the 110,000 that the market was looking for and the downward revision from 147,000 to 14,000 for the June numbers took the market lower. The S&P 500 fell -2.44% and the Nasdaq declined by -2.45% after all the macroeconomic data was digested. What I found interesting was that CME Group immediately changed their probabilities for a September rate cut to 93.6% and looking out to the end of 2026 the most likely scenario is that we will now get 225 bps of rate reductions. I am not seeing a lot of misses during earnings season, and it feels as if a lot of the EPS numbers are expanding. With the S&P 500 bouncing between 6,300 and 6,400 I think that it’s entirely likely that we could see a strong rally in the second half of 2025 especially if we get dovish commentary coming out of Jackson Hole later this month. The latest dip has allowed me to continue allocating capital at favorable levels and I am of the mindset to buy the dips and not sell the rips. I think we could be in a multi-year bull market as A.I. and robotics may lead to tremendous EPS growth. I am excited to continue building out the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio as there are still many opportunities in the market.

This was a difficult week in the markets and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio declined along with the market. After allocating $23,100 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 231 at $29,645.61. The account's profitability gave back all last weeks gains as it fell by -7.68% ($544.16) to $6,545.61, which is a return on invested capital of 28.34%. This week, I started a new position in the NEOS Bitcoin High Income ETF (BTCI), and added to Ares Capital (ARCC) and Flagstar Financial (FLAG). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $89.92 of dividend income in week 231. The combination of reinvesting the generated dividend income and making these investments increased the forward projected annualized dividend income by $31.80 (1.31%) to $2,461.19. The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has generated $4,563.28 of income since inception, which is 19.67% of the total capital I have allocated toward the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. The forward dividend income continues to expand, and the monthly income is trending higher. I am excited to see how Q3 unfolds as July was my strongest month of generating income and I think my updated projections for the 2nd half of 2025 are looking very good.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

The markets took a big dip this week and I am a believer in buying the dips as I have a long-term mindset. When I look at the profitability metrics of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio I am going based on the profitability metric since I am allocating fresh capital each week. On an account value basis, the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio only fell -2.49%, which is in-line with the market but I like to be harder on myself due to the additional capital. The profitability declined by -7.68% this week but the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is still in a strong position. I am still up $6,545.61 which is a return on capital of 28.34% and the forward dividend income being produced is $2,461.19. I believe that the portfolio is mitigating risk well and generating large amounts of recurring income despite market cycles. It will be interesting to see what happens in the next several weeks and I am excited about continuing to add capital before the Fed pivots to a lower rate environment.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • ETFs $828.76 (33.67%)
  • Equities $573.34 (23.38%)
  • CEFs $410.19 (16.67%)
  • REITs $395.42 (16.07%)
  • BDCs $251.47 (10.22%)

Investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. 2024 was a great year as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $1,623.14 in dividend income from 709 dividends. After 31 weeks in 2025, $1,383.60 of dividend income was produced, which is 85.24% of the total income from 2024. There were 461 positions that paid a dividend, which is 65.02% of the dividends generated in 2024.

There was $89.92 of dividend income generated this week, which increased the amount of dividend income I have generated on a weekly basis to $44.63. This is an increase of $13.42 or 42.99% YoY, and there have been 3 individual weeks when $100 of dividend income was exceeded. I am continuing to add capital toward positions that generate income on a monthly basis which should allow me to generate more weeks that exceed $100 in the future. In the first half of 2025 I generated $1,097.48 of dividend income, and I believe that there is a strong chance I can exceed $1,200 of dividend income being produced in the 2nd half of 2025.

July is over and it was the largest month of dividend income yet for the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio. There was $209.73 generated in July which marked the 2nd largest month of the year. August 1st was on Friday, and the month is starting off strong with $43.67 of dividend income being generated. August is going to be an interesting month because the trends are higher looking at the individual months of each quarter. In the 2nd month of Q1 there was $143.71 generated then $175.55 in the 2nd month of Q2. I think that I am going to be between $190 - $210 for the month of August with dividend income and I will also generate at least 1 month that falls between $225 - $250 of dividend income in 2025. As I continue to allocate capital the dividend snowball will continuously increase on a monthly basis.

My long-term goal is to have 75% or more of the portfolio generate at least 1 share per year from their dividend income. There are currently 44 positions accomplishing this, which is emphasized by the green section of the table below. These positions are expected to add roughly $413.28 of forward dividend income from those shares. Many positions are still lagging but I am making a strong effort allocating capital toward them so I can get at least 50% of the portfolio to accomplish this goal by the end of the year. My fixation on this is because it will allow me to advance the compounding impacts from the dividend snowball and it also makes sure that I am not investing in the same positions each week.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

ETFs are trending just above 19% and REITs have fallen below 19%, which I am happy about. It’s difficult for me to stay away from these sectors, as I believe that technology is going to heat up throughout earnings season and that there are still a lot of deals in the REIT segment. Individual equities now represent 39.08% of the Dividend Harvesting portfolio while generating 23.38% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 60.92% of the portfolio and generate 76.62% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5–10 years at this rate, if ever. I have been adding to energy companies as I believe there is a big opportunity there, and I will probably continue allocating capital toward energy positions as time progresses.

It’s crazy for me to watch Altria Group (MO) continue to increase over the 4.5% level after not adding a single share since February 2024. I am very happy that only 3 positions exceed 3% of the portfolio. It doesn’t look like much changed in the top-10 holdings except for where the NEOs Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI) is positioned. I wanted to add to Realty Income (O) after it got closer to 3% but after the jobs report I may add to several REITs before the September Fed meeting as I feel a rate cut is going to occur.

The top-10 positions didn’t change this week, but several did produce dividends. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $6,541.72, and they finished the week with a value of $8,817.24. This is a ROI of $2,275.53 (34.78%). There has been $1,198.56 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 18.32% of the initial investment into these positions. I'm now projecting that $631.77 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.66%. These positions represent 29.68% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 25.67% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 231 Additions

This week I started a new position in the NEOS Bitcoin High Income ETF (BTCI), and added to Ares Capital (ARCC) and Flagstar Financial (FLAG).

NEOS Bitcoin High Income ETF

  • I am a big fan of the NEOS ETFs and after reading up on BTCI I wanted to add it to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio
  • BTCI has generated an annualized yield of 22.74% and there are still 2 distributions to be generated in its first year of operations
  • Bitcoin (BTC-USD) is volatile and I think that BTCI will be able to capitalize on the fluctuations in its price to generate large amounts of income

Ares Capital

  • I wrote an article about ARCC in July (can be read here) where I discussed how ARCC was one of my favorite BDCs due to its net assets and net interest income (NII).
  • In Q1 ARCC allocated $3.5B in new investments and 92% were first lien senior secured loans, positioning ARCC for growth and stability in a lower-rate environment.
  • I believe that ARCC is going to do well in a lower rate environment as its expanding its asset base and could see its originations substantially increase

Flagstar Financial

  • I am down quite a bit on FLG and I wanted to reduce my price per share
  • I wrote an article on FLG recently (can be red here) where I discussed why I was still bullish on this regional bank
  • FLG was trading at a -37.73 discount to book value which was very interesting from a value perspective
  • When I looked at the forward EPS I felt it was a good value proposition as management felt it could generate adjusted EPS of $0.75-$0.80 for 2026 and $2.10-$2.20 for 2027

Week 232 Game plan

Next week I am looking to adding capital to Schlumberger Limited (SLB) and Bristol Myers Squibb (BMY).

Conclusion

This wasn’t the best week for the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio but my level of diversification helped mitigate risk. I believe that the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio is positioned for anything the market throws at it, and the S-curve in its forward annualized dividend income is just getting started. July ended up surpassing January in dividend income and I think I am on track to generate at least 1 month that exceeds $225 of dividend income. I am excited as we head into the fall as I believe the Fed is going to lower rates and the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio will do very well. I plan on allocating capital toward positions that I feel are undervalued and can benefit from a lower rate environment over the next several weeks. Please leave all your suggestions below as I enjoy interacting with everyone in the comment section.

data.title: CGDV: Great Fund To Own For Income And Gains - BUY
data.description:
Introduction

The Capital Group Dividend Value ETF (NYSEARCA:CGDV) is a fund that seeks to give investors consistent income that exceeds the average yield of the S&P 500 by focusing on companies with dividends currently or those which have the potential to pay dividends. They also hold some growth oriented names, where investors can have some upside exposure to growth names. Below are some details of the ETF taken from the prospectus.

  • Expense Ratio: 0.33%

  • Fund Assets: $20.4B

  • Volume: 3.8M

Their top holdings and exposure by sector and region are shown below.

We like the top holdings of the fund as we will discuss in the following section.

Top Holdings Review

Microsoft (MSFT)

This is a leading enterprise AI player and they recently reported strong earnings - revenue was up 18% on a year over year basis. The firm also stated that their cloud business produced over $75B in the 2025 fiscal year, which was up 34% from the prior year. We are pleased to see the enterprise computing business of MSFT grow and we believe that they are well positioned to continue being leaders in the enterprise AI space. Further, on the consumer side, we believe that as Windows 11 continues its takeover of the broader market as Windows 10 runs into its end of life this fall, there will be a large upgrade cycle that takes place in personal PCs as users shift their systems to the latest operating software. This should help to boost MSFT’s other business as well, in addition to their enterprise computing side of the house. The following image shows the growth of all of their businesses - and it's clear that not only is MSFT diversified, they are growing across the firm.

We are happy to see MSFT as the top holding of this fund for their growth, diversity of revenues, and strong future ambitions.

Broadcom (AVGO)

AVGO is a leading custom AI chip manufacturer, and they have big name partnerships with firms such as Google. We believe that AVGO represents a strong way to play the AI investment trend, as it allows investors to gain access to more than just standard GPUs that are used in data centers. Further, AVGO is consistently innovating in its products - as revealed in this press release from earlier this week. They are shipping their new Jericho4 ethernet fabric router, which is purpose built for the AI data center industry, to connect multiple data centers at once. They are building a strong portfolio of products that can perform in both high performance computing and AI use cases, and as a result, we view that AVGO remains a strong player in this field and one that is very worthy of investment. Their chips will allow data center operators to spread the power and usage requirements across more than one physical space, and this is where AVGO’s latest products come in. With the ability to connect numerous data centers across different regions, there will be an opportunity to increase data loads even further, leading to greater performance and heavier traffic loads for clients. This is ultimately a very strong positive for AVGO, and we believe that investors would be wise to look into this firm as an option for investing into the AI space in the future. Their products are critical to any data center and they have a diverse range of product options for many use cases.

Nvidia (NVDA)

As data centers continue to grow, there will be more and more need for AI chips to power the training and inference workloads that come through. NVDA products are leaders in this industry and they are led by a great CEO in Jensen Huang. As big tech firms have already spent $155B this year on AI capex alone, with more to go as forecasted, there is a large chunk of the pie that will go to NVDA. This trend, we believe, will continue for the foreseeable future due to the insatiable demand for AI computing that is trending now across all industries. As AMD CEO Lisa Su has commented regarding the increasing total addressable market for AI chips, this trend is going to lift all boats in the AI ocean but certainty will benefit NVDA as they are the largest player. We view NVDA as a pure play AI investment name, and despite having competition from the other chip players, they are still poised to benefit from the waves of AI investment that is coming. Even in China, as the chip restriction was recently lifted last month from the Trump administration, there has been demand for NVDA products, and this demand has been reflected in higher orders for the H20 chip from the manufacturer, Taiwan Semiconductor. NVDA is in a good spot to benefit from this AI investment cycle and they should remain leaders in the space. We are pleased to see them as a top holding in this ETF.

Fund Strategy

As Capital Group puts it, this fund “Seeks to produce consistent income that exceeds the average yield of the S&P 500 by focusing on companies that pay dividends or have the potential to pay dividends.” They go on to state “The fund's investment objectives are to produce income exceeding the average yield on U.S. stocks generally and to provide an opportunity for growth of principal consistent with sound common stock investing.”

Unpacking this, we see 3 major goals of the fund, in rough order of importance:

  1. Exceed the average yield of the S&P 500

  2. Invest in dividend paying companies (or companies that will likely pay dividends)

  3. Invest in companies that will grow in value

Let’s see how well the fund has met these goals.

Exceed the average yield of the S&P 500

The yield of CGDV is currently at 1.42% which is indeed higher than the S&P’s current yield of 1.22%. While it is true that the fund currently has a higher yield, it is not higher by much. On a $1000 investment, the difference equates to $2 per year. Historically, the yield of CGDV vs the S&P 500 are shown below (note: we use VOO as a proxy for the S&P 500 here).

We see that for much of the fund’s existence it has had a yield that was at or below that of the S&P 500.

Invest in dividend paying companies (or companies that will likely pay dividends)

We look at the top 10 holdings of CGDV and their respective dividend yields. For all of the companies, we source the TTM dividend yield from Seeking Alpha

Ticker

Yield

Percent of Fund

MSFT

0.62%

6.50%

AVGO

We see that the top 10 holdings have an average yield of 1.42% and a weighted average yield of 1.33%. We see that their goal of investing in dividend paying companies was technically met, but as discussed earlier, the yields are lackluster with the top 10 holdings only yielding 0.11% more than the S&P.

Invest in companies that will grow in value

To assess this, we look at the price return of the fund compared to the S&P 500. Note: We are looking at price return here not total return so dividends are excluded.

We see that CGDV has indeed had a higher price return than the S&P 500 over the last 3 years with a total gain of 73.61% compared to the S&P’s 51.96%.

Risks

AI Investment trend rotates elsewhere

There is the possibility that investors move away from AI firms and high flying computing names, and instead move capital into a more stable sector. Despite being a lower risk, we view that this should still be brought into consideration if the broader markets and economy suffer at the hands of poor macroeconomic news.

High Cost

The fund has an expense ratio of 0.33% compared to VOO for example that has an expense ratio of 0.03%. While it is common for an actively managed fund to have a higher expense ratio, we should keep in mind that the major goals of the fund are to beat the S&P 500 in both growth and dividends. Considering that the dividend yield of the fund is only 0.2% higher than the S&P’s yield, the 0.3% difference in expense ratio doesn’t seem as justifiable. Of course, this is ignoring the overall price return of the fund which has clearly been better than the S&Ps return over the last 3 years.

Conclusion

We are certainly big fans of the top holdings of this fund. They represent some of the best leaders in the big tech and AI space, and should have room to grow in the future as they are continuously innovating on their product portfolios and latest developments in AI.

We see that the fund has met the major investment goals that were laid forth in the prospectus which is great. We don’t love that the dividend yield on a “dividend value ETF” is not much higher than the S&Ps dividend, but are pleased with the overall performance of the fund when you factor in price growth.

We rate CGDV as a buy.

data.title: The London Company SMID Cap Vs. Russell 2500 Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Update

After the significant downturn in the first quarter, U.S. equities experienced a double-digit increase in 2Q. The broader market, as measured by the Russell 3000 Index, increased 11.0%. Volatility surged in early April following new U.S. tariff announcements aimed at reshaping global trade. A double-digit, risk-on rally ensued following a temporary pause in tariff escalations, widespread optimism around enterprise AI, and a healthy earnings outlook. Additionally, improving sentiment, a healthy labor market, and expectations of future Federal Reserve rate cuts further supported the upward momentum, even amidst geopolitical tensions and mixed economic signals. Growth styles led Value, and Large Caps outperformed Small Caps. Turning to market factors, Growth and Volatility posted the strongest returns, and most Momentum factors outperformed. Value and Yield factors had a negative impact, while Quality factors were mixed.

Key Performance Takeaways

  • The London Company SMID portfolio decreased 0.6% (-0.8% net) during the second quarter vs. an 8.6% increase in the Russell 2500 Index. Sector allocation was a tailwind to relative performance, offset by stock allocation.

  • The SMID portfolio came up short of our 85-90% upside capture expectations in 2Q. Our exposure to Lower Volatility and Quality factors flipped from a tailwind in 1Q to a headwind in 2Q, as high beta and momentum driven rallied sharply following tariff de-escalation. Pockets of weakness across several holdings further exacerbated relative performance headwinds amidst the risk-on rally. We elected to maintain our position in a few of the weaker positions, reflecting our long-term conviction in the businesses. Fortunes can change quickly with a high conviction portfolio, and we remain confident in the operating metrics of portfolio companies. While the Index is broader down cap, the absence of owning the top performers contributed to nearly 20% of the underperformance this quarter. The recent strength of some of the riskier, more speculative Mid & Small Caps names is not driven by fundamentals nor is it sustainable longer-term.

Top 3 Contributors to Relative Performance

  • NewMarket Corporation (NEU) – NEU outperformed this quarter, fueled by strong AMPAC revenue growth from better volume and product mix, despite weaker Petroleum Additives results. The planned capacity expansion for the AMPAC business signals robust demand for rocket additives in a stable, oligopolistic market. Management prioritizes debt reduction and shareholder returns through dividends and buybacks.

  • Armstrong World Industries, Inc. (AWI) – AWI shares outperformed in the quarter due to beating expectations, driven by increased volumes from recent acquisitions and an increase in pricing. We continue to like AWI for its consistent execution, strong financials, leading market share and persistent moats through its exclusivity agreements and warranties.

  • AerCap Holdings (AER) – AER delivered strong performance this quarter, bolstered by solid Q1 2025 results and a favorable UK court ruling on $1B in insurance claims related to the Russia-Ukraine War. With the largest aircraft portfolio in a seller’s market, AerCap is well-positioned to grow book value per share through strategic asset sales and stock buybacks, capitalizing on robust market dynamics.

Top 3 Detractors from Relative Performance

  • Murphy USA, Inc. (MUSA) – MUSA was a bottom performer after reporting softer market share gains in Q1 due to lower retail fuel prices. With that said, the long-term components of our thesis remain intact: structurally higher industry fuel margins, everyday low price driving profitable market share gains, and value-creating capital allocation.

  • Saia, Inc. (SAIA) – SAIA remained weak this quarter as the lingering “freight recession” drags on for almost its third year. We remain convicted in our ownership of SAIA due to its solid balance sheet and strong network growth plan. We believe the company could be a multi-year compounder, assuming the freight industry returns to normal demand patterns.

  • White Mountains Insurance Group Ltd (WTM) – WTM was a bottom name after facing industry headwinds. For U.S. P&C insurers, performance was mundane in 2Q, whereas the rest of the market had a rebound. We remain confident in the company’s ability to deliver outsized growth in book value per share over time through prudent capital allocation.

Sector Influence

We are bottom-up stock pickers, but sector exposures influenced relative performance as follows:

  • What Helped: Underweight in both Energy and Real Estate (weaker performing sectors)

  • What Hurt: Overweight in Consumer Staples and Materials (weaker performing sectors)

Trades During the Quarter

  • Exited: Cannae Holdings, Inc. (CNNE) – Underperformed in recent years and was only 1% of the portfolio. Our initial thesis centered on Bill Foley’s ability to create value and the most valuable asset of CNNE was Dun & Bradstreet. That investment has not worked out and CNNE shares have lagged the broader market. We elected to sell the remaining position.

  • Reduced: AerCap Holdings – We trimmed AER on strength. The stock has significantly outperformed the broader market since early 2021. We remain confident in the outlook for air travel and for AER to benefit by leasing planes to the largest airlines.

  • Increased: Saia, Inc. – With recent weakness and a smaller beginning weight in the portfolio, we felt it was prudent to add to the shares and make it a 3% position.

  • Increased: Zebra Technologies Corporation (ZBRA) – Added to the position following weakness in the shares early in the year. Concerns around tariffs and weaker tech spending provided a more attractive valuation.

  • Initiated: Somnigroup International Inc (SGI) – Formerly Tempur Sealy, SGI is the combined company of Tempur Sealy the acquired Mattress Firm. SGI is the clear leader in the bedding industry, and the company is gaining market share while generating robust free cash flow. Strong brand equity, solid management execution, and vertical integration from the acquisition support our investment thesis. Currently, the bedding market is weak with volumes near a 30-year low. Higher mortgage rates and rising home prices may limit unit sales in the short term. Longer term, we believe that an eventual industry rebound, synergies from the acquisition, and greater innovation are keys to the company’s success. We have owned SGI in the past and just sold it from the Small Cap portfolio due to its rising market cap following the acquisition of Mattress Firm.

Looking Ahead

There is elevated uncertainty as we start the second half of 2025 with a high likelihood of greater tariffs being announced in the weeks ahead. Consumer confidence has declined recently due to the risks of additional tariffs, but the broader economic data still supports growth in the near term, although growth is decelerating. While we are not predicting a recession, the odds of a recession have increased. In terms of monetary policy, the Fed appears to be on a steady course for two or three rate cuts later in the year. The effect of tariffs on both inflation and the broader economy could change those plans, though.

In terms of the equity market, the S&P 500 is back to the expensive, concentrated territory in which it began the year. Meanwhile, the macro risk backdrop has deteriorated since the start of 2025, including labor market softening, ongoing tariff uncertainty, debt headwinds, geopolitical tensions, and unclear inflation trends. Equity returns in the near term may be modest, with shareholder yield (dividends, share repurchases, debt reduction) comprising a significant percentage of the total return from equities. We believe our high-Quality, low-Volatility orientation positions us well for an environment of elevated policy risks and fragile global growth. We remain rooted to our long-term, fundamental investment approach, focusing on company quality, sustainable returns on capital, and resilience across economic scenarios.

Annualized Returns

As of 6/30/2025

Composite Creation/Inception Date: March 31, 2009

Composite Definition: The Small-Mid Cap strategy is an extension of our Small Cap strategy with weighted market capitalization higher than our Small Cap portfolio, and is within the market capitalization ranges of the major domestic small to mid-cap indices.
